coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of sulfur-chlorine-doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots from leachate membrane concentrate as a selective probe for Pd2+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Shuocheng
Zhang, Hao
Li, Haiyan
Pei, Yuansheng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, humic acid-based sulfur-chlorine-doped carbon quantum dots (L-CQDs) were synthesized by using a polyethylene glycol (PEG)-assisted hydrothermal technique using landfill leachate concentrate (LLC) as a carbon source. The prepared L-CQDs contain many oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface with a small average particle size of 4–6 nm, resulting in excellent hydrophilicity and dispensability. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum shows that the characteristic peak for humic acid for LLC is redshifted after adding PEG as an inactivator, indicating that L-CQDs are synthesized by PEG encapsulation and linkage. The optimum condition for preparing L-CQDs involves the addition of 0.5 g PEG (MW = 2000) into 20 mL LLC and reaction at 200 °C for 5 h, with the optimal L-CQD quantum yield reaching 5.32%. L-CQDs were used to prepare fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of palladium ions (Pd2+). The obtained nanoprobe shows admirable performance in detecting Pd2+, with high selectivity, excellent water solubility and stability. The fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe is selectively quenched through static quenching by Pd2+ in the concentration range of 5–40 μmol L−1. This work provides a simple, easy, and fast detection method for Pd2+ and opens up a way for the resource utilization of LLC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Landfill leachate membrane concentrate
Carbon quantum dots
Palladium-ion detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence probed based determination of Pd2+
Optimization of synthesis conditions for the L-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of L-CQDs. (a) TEM image of L-CQDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image of L-CQDs (inset shows the Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT) image and the lattice of L-CQDs). (c) Energy Dispersive spectrum of the L-CQDs.
Morphological properties of L-CQDs. (a) FTIR spectrum of L-CQDs. (b) XRD pattern of L-CQDs. (c) Survey XPS spectrum of L-CQDs. (d) The deconvolution of C 1s spectrum of L-CQDs.

Optical properties of L-CQDs. (a) and (c) Normalized 3D FL spectra of L-CQDs. (b) and (d) Ultraviolet and Visible (UV) spectrum of L-CQDs. [L-CQDs of (a) and (b) were heated by 20 mL LLC alone at 200 °C for 5 h; L-CQDs of (c) and (d) were heated by 20 mL LLC + PEG 1.0 g at 200 °C for 5 h].

Effect of different measurement conditions on FL intensity of L-CQDs (heated by 20 mL LLC + PEG 1.0 g at 200 °C for 5 h). (a) Histogram of FL intensity and Em corresponding to different Ex (300 nm–375 nm) of L-CQDs. (b) FL intensity curves of L-CQDs corresponding to different Ex (300 nm–375 nm). (c) Different pH values (2–11.5) on the Ex 340 nm. (d) Different ratios of buffer solution (0.01 mL–8.00 mL) on the Ex 340 nm.
Effect of different PEG conditions on FL intensity of L-CQDs. (a)Different PEG molecular weights (1000 D, 2000 D, 4000 D). (b) Different PEG addition quality (0.5 g–5.0 g).
Effect of different hydrothermal conditions on FL intensity of L-CQDs. (a) Different heating temperatures (100°C–300 °C) for 5 h. (b) Different heating times (1 h–9 h) at 200 °C.

Quenching L-CQDs by heavy metal ions. (a) Histogram of FL quenching ratio (F/F0) of different heavy metal ion solutions (Hg, Fe, Cr, …, Na) with concentration of 20 μmol L−1 added to L-CQDs solution respectively. (b) Histogram of FL quenching ratio (F/F0) of L-CQDs after different heavy metal interference solutions with ten times concentration (200 μmol L−1) were added respectively.

Pd2+ quenching L-CQDs. (a) FL intensity curves of L-CQDs solution after different c(Pd2+) (0 μmol L−1 -200 μmol L−1) were added respectively at the optimal Ex (340 nm); (b) FL quenching ratio of L-CQDs solution after different c(Pd2+) (0 μmol L−1-200 μmol L−1); (c) Linear fitting interval diagram of Pd2+ quenching L-CQDs solution.
The curves of fluorescence lifetime and the calculation of average fluorescence lifetime of L-CQDs before and after adding Pd2+ (20 μmol L−1).

Probe into fluorescence quenching mechanism of L-CQDs. (a) UV image of the L-CQDs before and after adding Pd2+ (20 μmol L−1). (b) zeta potential diagram of the L-CQDs before and after adding Pd2+ (20 μmol L−1).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of sulfur-chlorine-doped fluorescent carbon quantum dots from leachate membrane concentrate as a selective probe for Pd2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Energy Conservation & Sustainable Urban and Rural Development Provincial and Ministry Co-construction Collaboration Innovation Centre, Beijing University of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Beijing, 100044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Energy Conservation & Sustainable Urban and Rural Development Provincial and Ministry Co-construction Collaboration Innovation Centre
Beijing University of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Energy Conservation & Sustainable Urban and Rural Development Provincial and Ministry Co-construction Collaboration Innovation Centre, Beijing University of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Beijing 100044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Water and Sediment Sciences, Ministry of Education, School of Environment, Beijing Normal University, Beijing, 100875, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Water and Sediment Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Laboratory of Water and Sediment Sciences, Ministry of Education, School of Environment, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, humic acid-based sulfur-chlorine-doped carbon quantum dots (L-CQDs) were synthesized by using a polyethylene glycol (PEG)-assisted hydrothermal technique using landfill leachate concentrate (LLC) as a carbon source. The prepared L-CQDs contain many oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface with a small average particle size of 4–6 nm, resulting in excellent hydrophilicity and dispensability. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum shows that the characteristic peak for humic acid for LLC is redshifted after adding PEG as an inactivator, indicating that L-CQDs are synthesized by PEG encapsulation and linkage. The optimum condition for preparing L-CQDs involves the addition of 0.5 g PEG (MW = 2000) into 20 mL LLC and reaction at 200 °C for 5 h, with the optimal L-CQD quantum yield reaching 5.32%. L-CQDs were used to prepare fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of palladium ions (Pd2+). The obtained nanoprobe shows admirable performance in detecting Pd2+, with high selectivity, excellent water solubility and stability. The fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe is selectively quenched through static quenching by Pd2+ in the concentration range of 5–40 μmol L−1. This work provides a simple, easy, and fast detection method for Pd2+ and opens up a way for the resource utilization of LLC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, due to the application of drugs, chemicals and coatings in agriculture, casting and industry, the amount of human exposure to heavy metals has increased exponentially, and heavy metals have become a threat to human health worldwide [1,2]. In particular, palladium ions (Pd2+), which are a common heavy metal in daily life or industrial production, are widely used in catalysts, dentistry, medical equipment, fuel cells and the pharmaceutical industry because of their high temperature resistance, corrosion resistance and strong practicability [3,4]. However, industrial wastes containing Pd2+ will be discharged into the environment, which can cause serious harm to human health through the accumulation of biological chains in water and soil [5–7]. At present, the conventional methods for detecting Pd2+ in water include atomic absorption spectroscopy, atomic emission spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma‒mass spectrometry [8]. These methods can be used to accurately detect Pd2+ content, but the required instruments and equipment are expensive, and the sample pretreatment process is complicated. Therefore, it is very important to develop a simple and rapid innovative method to detect Pd2+. Recently, fluorescent probe detection has been increasingly used for heavy metal detection [9]. Among them, fluorescent probes prepared using carbon dots have become the focus of the scientific community because of their high sensitivity, strong anti-interference property and simple operation [10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are spherical carbon nanoparticles with fluorescent properties [11–13]. CQDs inherit the advantages of fluorescent macromolecules and traditional semiconductor quantum dots, such as adjustable luminous colour, low preparation cost, and good water solubility [14,15]. CQDs also have the advantages of light bleaching resistance, a wide excitation wavelength range, nontoxicity and environmental friendliness. CQDs are widely used in photoelectric devices, photocatalysis, analysis and detection, biological imaging and other fields [16–20]. Notably, the fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) and sensing performance can be improved by doping, especially by the co-doping of heteroatoms (such as chlorine and sulfur in CQDs) [21-23]. Therefore, developing a simple and effective method to prepare chlorine and sulfur co-doped CQDs remains a concern. In addition, there are many methods to prepare CQDs at this stage, such as the microwave method, hydrothermal method, electrochemical method, laser ablation and chemical ablation [24–26]. The hydrothermal method is the most important preparation method at present because of its simple operation, high quantum yield and low cost [27]. Conventional carbon sources used to prepare carbon dots include glucose and citric acid. In recent years, people have begun to pay attention to the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs by using inexpensive carbon sources, such as milk, saccharin, radish and some organic pollutants. These CQDs have been used for the detection and biological imaging of different heavy metal ions [28–31].

Landfill membrane concentrated solution (LLC) is high-concentration organic wastewater produced by landfill or incineration of municipal garbage followed by technological treatment [32]. Some conventional treatment methods of LLC, such as coagulation sedimentation and evaporation concentration, either have poor treatment effects or harsh use conditions, and the treatment cost is extremely high [29–31]. Considering that the main components of LLC are humic acid, inorganic salts and some ammonia nitrogen compounds [32], LLC contains a large number of aromatic domains needed to prepare carbon dots and contains polar functional groups such as alcohols and carboxylic acids. These characteristics inspire us to use LLC as a carbon source to produce self-doped CQDs and provide new ideas for the resource utilization of LLC.

In this work, L-CQDs were prepared by the PEG-assisted hydrothermal method, and LLC was used as a carbon source to detect Pd2+. To the best of our knowledge, the preparation of CQDs by LLC has not yet been reported. Moreover, the feasibility of L-CQDs for the detection of Pd2+ ions is further proven. L-CQDs show a selective response when measuring Pd2+ in an aqueous solution with multiple interfering metal ions and can be used for the trace detection of Pd2+. According to the principle of static quenching, an inexpensive and efficient L-CQD probe was prepared, which provides a new idea for the reuse of LLC.

LLC was taken from a domestic waste landfill in Tianjin, China. LLC contains a large amount of humic acid, ammonia nitrogen, and inorganic salts and has the characteristics of a typical late leachate and membrane concentrated solution [33]. Anhydrous ethanol, quinine sulfate, polyethylene glycol (PEG) (MW = 2000), and dialysis bags (MW3000 Da) were all purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Science Company. All chemical reagents and medicines were analytically pure. Ultra-pure water (R > 18 MΩ cm−1, prepared using a Milli-Q water purifier) was used for the experiment.

First, 10–20 mL of LLC and 0.5–2.0 g of PEG2000 were added into the polytetrafluoroethylene lining, and ultrasonic treatment was performed for 5–15 min to obtain a uniformly dispersed solution. Then, the solution was placed into a 100 mL reaction kettle, heated in a muffle furnace at 100–300 °C for 1–9 h, and naturally cooled to room temperature. A disposable syringe was used to suck the initial carbon dot solution and filter it with a 0.22-μm filter membrane. The L-CQD solution was put into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3000 and dialyzed in ultrapure water for 48 h. The volume of dialysate was set to 50 ml, and the dialysate was placed into a refrigerator for later use. The solid CQD particles were obtained by rotary evaporation and freeze-drying.

UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometry were performed using a Hach DR6000 and Hach F-4600, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using a Hermo F200XS at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured using an X pert pro MPD (Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer with potassium bromide (KBr) pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an Escslab 250Xi. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed using a LifeSpec spectrometer (Edinburgh, FLS1000).

The quantum yields for L-CQDs were determined by the quinine sulfate reference method [28,34]. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid was selected as the fluorescence standard substance. The integral fluorescence intensity of dilute L-CQD solution and dilute quinine sulfate solution at the same excitation wavelength and the absorbance of excitation light at this wavelength (the absorbance was adjusted to below 0.1) were measured [35–37]. The quantum yield for the L-CQDs was calculated by using the following formula:
(1)
Y u
=
Y s
A s
A u
F u
F s
n u
2
n s
2
where u and s represent CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively,
Y
is the fluorescence quantum yield,
A
is the absorbance at a specific wavelength, and
F
is the integral fluorescence intensity. The integral area of the fluorescence was used in the calculation, and
n
is the refractive index of the solvent. Considering that the solvents for L-CQDs and quinine sulfate standard materials are dilute solutions, the refractive index is almost 1.33, which can be reduced in the calculation. The fluorescence quantum yield for L-CQDs can be obtained by substituting the above values into the formula.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence probed based determination of Pd2+
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching effect for the fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs at room temperature was studied. In a typical experiment, 1 mL of L-CQD solution was added to a 5 mL centrifuge tube, and then different volumes of standard metal ions (Pd2+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Cr3+, and Ba2+) were added [38]. Finally, the volume was held constant with a Britten-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH = 7) to 5 mL, and the concentration of metal ions in each centrifuge tube was adjusted to 20 μmol L−1. The fluorescence intensity of each metal ion buffer system was measured at the optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and the selective quenching effect of metal ions on L-CQDs and the fluorescence quenching mechanism were discussed.

TEM was used to observe the microscopic appearance and particle size of L-CQDs. Fig. 1 shows that the prepared L-CQDs have a uniform particle size and good dispersability, and most of them are spherical with a particle size of 4–6 nm. High-resolution TEM photographs show that the lattice spacing of the synthesized L-CQDs is 0.309 nm, and it can be observed from the energy spectrum that carbon is the main element, with doping of S and Cl elements. S and Cl enter the continuous sp2 hybrid carbon structure to form clear lattice defects, thus effectively improving the fluorescence quantum yield and potential catalytic properties of the prepared L-CQDs [39,40].

The surface functional groups of the prepared L-CQDs were characterized by FTIR. Fig. 2 a shows that L-CQDs contain a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. An absorption peak band can be observed in the wavenumber range of 300–3700 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and the stretching vibration of N–H [41]. The stretching vibration peak at 1647 cm−1 indicates that a C=C double bond is present in L-CQDs [42]. The stretching vibration peak for C–H is observed at 2884 cm−1, and the absorption peak at 1114 cm−1 is attributed to the tensile vibration of C–O [43]. The oxygen-containing functional groups lead to excellent hydrophilic characteristics for the prepared L-CQDs compared with the characteristics of poor water-solubility for the graphite carbon materials such as graphene and carbon nanotubes [44], which make them well dispersible in water and are beneficial to the stability of fluorescent probe testing.

The structural changes in LLC during transformation into L-CQDs were further characterized by XRD. For CQDs with smaller particle sizes, a broad peak with a peak value of 21.2° usually indicates the formation of carbon nuclei in CQD synthesis [45]. As shown in Fig. 2b, the characteristic peaks for PEG appear at 19.1° and 23.2°, and most of the characteristic peaks for PEG2000 are retained, while the typical broad peaks are concentrated at 21.2°. The above XRD patterns show that PEG2000, as an inactivator, can promote the synthesis of L-CQDs. The oxidative decomposition of PEG is very difficult, and its properties are relatively stable; thus, PEG will not produce redundant by products to interfere with the reaction system [46,47].

The chemical structure of the L-CQD solution was analysed by XPS. Fig. 2c shows that the structure is mainly composed of C and O elements, and the relative contents of C and O are 73.2% and 25.9%, respectively, while the relative content of Cl, S and N is 0.3%. The material was characterized by a high-resolution spectrogram and standardized peak separation, and the existing form of carbon on the surface of L-CQDs was determined. Fig. 2d shows that the L-CQDs have three clear carbon structure peaks: sp2-hybridized C=C (284.8 eV), C–O–C/C–OH (286.1 eV), and C=O (288.2 eV). Therefore, the surface contains a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, which is also consistent with the results obtained from infrared absorption spectroscopy [48].

Fig. 3 shows the three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum and ultraviolet absorption spectrum of a solution prepared before and after the addition of the PEG inactivator. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum shows that humic acid shows only one characteristic peak (rectangular box in Fig. 3a) without the addition of PEG. After the addition of PEG, the characteristic peak for humic acid shows a lateral shift, and a characteristic peak suspected to be due to L-CQDs (round box in Fig. 3c) is generated [49]. As shown in Fig. 3b and d, after adding the PEG inactivator, the characteristic peak for humic acid disappears at 300–350 nm [50], but a weak typical absorption shoulder peak appears at 280 nm. This shoulder peak is attributed to the n-π* transition in the sp2 region, and it is a typical absorption peak of the conjugated π structure of aromatic compounds [51]. Meanwhile, there is a very significant terminal absorption. In the wavelength range below 400 nm, the absorbance increases significantly with decreasing wavelength. The absorbance above 400 nm slowly decreases with increasing wavelength and extends to above 600 nm. Therefore, the prepared L-CQD solution contains a large number of unsaturated chromophore structures, such as aromatic rings [52].

As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the optimum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength for the synthesized L-CQDs are 340 nm and 434.2 nm, respectively. The buffer system is composed of BR buffer solution and L-CQD solution, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at different pH values and concentrations. Fig. 4c shows that the fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs increases with increasing pH value from 2.0 to 6.0 under the optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm; nevertheless, the intensity begins to decrease gradually as the pH value is increased above 10.0. A pH of 6.0 is determined to be the best condition for achieving a high fluorescence intensity for L-CQDs. The strong reaction of L-CQDs to pH changes is attributed to the protonation of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface [53]. Since a change in pH can lead to protonation or deprotonation of the central fluorescence emission point, the fluorescence intensity will significantly decrease under acidic and alkaline conditions. Fig. 4d shows that when the ratio of BR buffer solution to L-CQDs is 7:3, the fluorescence intensity emitted by L-CQDs is the highest under the optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm. A high concentration of CQD solution will lead to low intensity, which is called the concentration quenching effect for CQDs [54].

Fig. 5 a shows the fluorescence intensity for L-CQD solutions with different PEG molecular weights, it can be seen that different molecular weights of PEG have certain effects on the prepared L-CQDs. The reason of the phenomenon is that in the synthesis of L-CQDs from LLC and PEG, the reactions mainly went through an “oxidized decomposition-passivate”. For the oxidized decomposition to occur, a certain threshold of temperature and reaction duration was required, which was closely related to the M.W. of PEG used: for PEG with low M.W (PEG 1000), relatively low temperature and short reaction time could trigger the PEG passivation of LLC to L-CQDs; and elevated temperature and prolonged reaction tend to over carbonize L-CQDs and thus reduce their fluorescence. On the other hand, for PEG with large M.W. (PEG 4000), relatively high temperature and long reaction time would be required to trigger the oxidized decomposition and the following passivation of LLC to L-CQDs; if the temperature was low and reaction time was short, there may be no L-CQDs formed. Fig. 5b shows that L-CQDs have the best excitation wavelength of approximately 430 nm under an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and the intensity first increases and then decreases with the addition of PEG. Fig. 5b shows that with increasing PEG recruitment, the fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases. For a PEG dosage of 1.0 g, the fluorescence intensity reaches its highest value, and the position of the emission peak remains almost unchanged, which is probably due to PEG as an inactivator improving the surface defects in the L-CQDs. For example, compared with semiconductor quantum dots, there are many nonradiative composite defects on the surface, which can be greatly improved by PEG modification. Moreover, the L-CQDs themselves will agglomerate, which will lead to a redshift of the emission peak and a reduction in the quantum yield. Adding the long-chain molecule PEG can play a role in dispersion and enhance the fluorescence to a certain extent.

Meanwhile, nanocarbon particles with large molecular weights are formed by the polymerization of small-molecule organic matter based on the bottom-up hydrothermal method [55]. PEG has the function of coating particles and connecting particles. The former limits the growth of individual particles to form macromolecular biochar, while the latter makes it easier for particles to aggregate to form nanocarbon dots. However, with an increase in PEG, the reaction space decreases, and PEG itself has the characteristics of physiological inertia and mildness. Too much PEG will lead to insufficient hydrothermal reaction and a decrease in fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions for the L-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: After determining the optimal ratio for the raw materials (1.0 g PEG: 20 mL LLC), the heating time for the hydrothermal reaction was further explored. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, when the heating time is extended from 1 to 5 h, the fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs first increases before decreasing slightly as the reaction time is further extended to 9 h. The change trend of FL intensity from low to high heating temperature is to decrease first, then increase and finally decrease. The reason for this phenomenon is that low reaction temperature will lead to the failure of hydrothermal reaction, while the fluorescence properties of humic acid in LLC will lead to higher FL intensity. With the increase of heating temperature, humic acid will be converted into intermediates or L-CQDs, which will lead to the decrease of FL intensity. However, with the further increase of temperature, LLC was fully converted into L-CQDs, which led to the increase of FL intensity. The fluorescence from CQDs prepared by the bottom-up method is reported to mainly depend on the nuclear state (carbonized core-induced PL) and the molecular state (incorporated or attached fluorescent molecular fragments-induced PL). The fluorescence is dominated by the molecular state (with high fluorescence QY) in the early stages, but the core state (with low fluorescence QY) dominates in the later stages, where molecular fragments are gradually carbonized to form a carbon core under high temperature and prolonged reaction [56]. Accordingly, the following can be used to explain the experimental observations for the L-CQD fluorescence intensity: The molecular stage predominates the fluorescence of L-CQDs from 1 h to 5 h or from 100 °C to 200 °C. However, as the reaction times are prolonged to 9 h, a large number of fluorescent molecular fragments are carbonized to form the carbon core, and the fluorescence from L-CQDs is dominated by the core state, leading to weaker intensity. Because excessive carbonization proceeds rapidly at 300 °C due to the high temperature, the fluorescence intensity is decreased as reactions run further. The L9(34) orthogonal test was carried out for the above three main influencing factors. The experimental results are shown in Table 1 .

As shown in Table 1, K1, K2 and K3 represent the sum of the CQD fluorescence intensity for each factor level, while
K 1
‾
,
K 2
‾
and
K 3
‾
represent the average of the CQD fluorescence intensity for each factor level. The primary and secondary order that affects the intensity of the L-CQDs is heating time (Factor C), heating temperature (Factor B), and PEG content (Factor A). Therefore, the best combination of factors is A1B2C2, that is, the best technical conditions are PEG 0.5 g, reaction temperature 200 °C, and holding time 5 h, and the best quantum yield is calculated to be 5.32%.

The selectivity of synthesized L-CQDs to other cations was studied. Twenty μmol L−1 solutions of different cations were added to L-CQDs at the same concentration, and the changes in their fluorescence intensity were measured. Fig. 7 a shows the fluorescence intensity of the solution measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The addition of Pd2+ significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity of the solution. In contrast, with the addition of other cations, the fluorescence intensity slightly decreases or hardly changes. Fig. 7b shows that most interfering ions have no obvious influence on the intensity of L-CQDs under interference at 10 times the concentration, which indicates that the prepared L-CQDs have good selectivity for Pd2+.

The effect of different concentrations of Pd2+ on the fluorescence intensity of L-CQDs was investigated to determine the possibility of using L-CQDs as a fluorescent probe to determine Pd2+. Fig. 8 a shows the change in the fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence intensity for the L-CQD solution at different concentrations of Pd2+. With an increase in Pd2+ concentration from 0 μmol L−1 to 200 μmol L−1, the fluorescence intensity of the solution gradually decreases, and the optimum emission wavelength shows an obvious redshift under the condition of a higher Pd2+ concentration. On the one hand, the synthesized material shows a wide fluorescence emission peak; on the other hand, this may be related to a change in the binding site between Pd2+ and the L-CQD surface [57]. Fig. 8b and c shows a linear interval in the concentration range of 5–40 μmol L−1. The above interval was linearly fitted, as shown in Fig. 8c. In the concentration range of 5–40 μmol L−1, the linear equation is Y = 0.012X+0.18 (R2 = 0.9945), and the detection limit is 0.1 μmol L−1. In the visibility range, the fluorescence intensity for L-CQDs shows a good linear relationship with Pd2+, which is expected to be used for further actual water sample detection.

Previous experiments have proven that Pd2+ ions can significantly quench fluorescent probes prepared using L-CQDs. The quenching behaviour of CQDs can be described by two mechanisms: dynamic quenching and static quenching [58]. Dynamic quenching occurs due to the collision between a fluorophore and quencher, in which fluorescence quenching depends on the time and concentration. In static quenching, a ground-state complex is formed by an interaction between the fluorophore and functional groups of the quencher, and linear correlation quenching occurs [59,60]. The quenching mechanism for L-CQDs due to Pd2+ was studied by measuring the zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime. Fluorescence lifetime evaluation can usually be used to determine the possible mechanism type between CQDs and quenchers. In this work, the fluorescence lifetime was determined in the presence and absence of Pd2+, and the results are shown in Fig. 9 . The weighted average lifetime of L-CQDs before and after adding Pd2+ ions is 2.1 ns and 2.4 ns, respectively, and the fitting degrees are all above 95%. The fluorescence lifetime of L-CQDs remains unchanged, which confirms the coordination or chelation between Pd2+ ions and various functional groups on the L-CQD surface and the fluorescence quenching caused by the static quenching mechanism.

The Stern-Vollmer equation [61] under environmental conditions was studied to explore the quenching mechanism for L-CQDs:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 + K sv
[ Q ]
where Ksv is the Stern-Voltammer quenching constant, F
0 and F are the PL intensities of L-CQDs in the absence and presence of Pd2+, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher (Pd2+). The bimolecular quenching constant (K
q ) equation K
q = Ksv/T
0 was calculated according to the slope of the Stern-Volmer curve (Ksv = 1.63 × 106 M−1), where T
0 is the fluorescence lifetime of L-CQDs without Pd2+. According to the maximum value of the dynamic quenching (2 × 1010 M−1s−1), the relative maximum value of K
q (7.62 × 1014 M−1s−1) indicates the involvement of a static mechanism and the formation of a ground-state complex, which arises from the interaction between the L-CQD surface functional groups and Pd2+. In addition, the L-CQD solution before and after adding Pd2+ was analysed by UV and zeta potential analysis. Fig. 10 a shows that the intensity of the absorption peak at 280 nm slightly increases after the addition of Pd2+, which can be attributed to the formation of a fairly stable complex between the nanoprobe and Pd2+ [62]. Fig. 10b shows that the zeta potential decreases from −8.77 mV to −16.75 mV with the addition of Pd2+. Since Pd2+ can prevent the protonation of amine groups on the surface of L-CQDs, the zeta potential becomes more negative, and the fluorescence characteristics are diminished [52]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism involves static quenching between L-CQDs and Pd2+.

Novel sulfur-chlorine-doped carbon dots were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method with LLC as the carbon source and PEG as the passivator.
(1)
The prepared L-CQDs show a clear lattice structure with a narrow size distribution (4–6 nm) and excellent luminescent properties. The surface of the L-CQDs contains a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups and is doped with sulfur and chlorine. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for L-CQDs are 340 nm and 430 nm, respectively.
(2)
The preparation conditions were optimized by single-factor and orthogonal tests. According to the orthogonal test, the key factor is the quantity of PEG passivator added. The optimal preparation conditions are PEG 0.5 g, heating temperature 200 °C, and heating time 5 h, and the optimal quantum yield is 5.32%.
(3)
L-CQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for detecting palladium ions with high sensitivity and selectivity. In the presence of 15 different kinds of interfering metal ions, the fluorescent probe and Pd2+ in aqueous solution are statically quenched, with a sensitive selective detection response observed over a linear concentration range of 5–40 μmol L−1. The method provides a new idea for the recycling of LLC and the rapid detection of Pd2+ in water.

Shuocheng Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Hao Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Haiyan Li: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yuansheng Pei: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Major Science and Technology Program for Water Pollution Control and Treatment (Project no. 2018ZX07110004), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project no. 51879012, 5217100174), the BUCEA Post Graduate Innovation Project (PG2022050).

J. Environ. Chem. Eng.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous stripping determination of cadmium and lead ions based on the N-doped carbon quantum dots-graphene oxide hybrid
Sensor. Actuator. B Chem.
